free_text: High resolution measurements of gamma rays produced
AN-400 positive ion accelerator at Lund.
Neutron energy of 15 MeV. The neutrons incident on the
sample (angular interval 20 to 70 deg) had n-energy
gamma radiation with energy less than 7.5 MeV.
The resolution of the detector system was about 7 keV
at 1 MeV and about 20 keV at 6 MeV.
A B-F(3) counter used to monitor the n-flux.
the 4.43 MeV g-line from C12(n,n'g)C12 at an incident
neutron energy of 14.8 MeV is given by I.L.Morgan
report for period ending August 1,1964,P.156, Texas
Distance between target and sample = about 10 cm.
Detector placed about 50 cm from the sample, angles
between the direction of the neutrons and that of the
gamma rays to the detector were varied 50 to 110 deg.
Neutrons by (d-t) reaction and incident on the sample,
subtended an angle between 20 and 70 deg relative to
The lower limits for cross section determination were
about 3 mb/Sr at 0.5 MeV, 1 mb/Sr at 1 MeV and 0.5
mb/sr at 3 MeV for Fe, Ni and Cu samples. For natural
Pb and Bi the corresponding cross section values were
The stability of the detector system was +- 1/2
channel(1 channel = 1.6 keV), better than earlier exp.
Metal, circular discs about 3 cm thick with a dia-
meter of 15 cm of natural sample.
Measured gamma yields were corrected for multiple
scattering by Monte Carlo calculation. The ratio of
the number of neutrons scattered non-elastically
after the second or third collision to the number of
neutrons scattered non-elastically at the first
Gamma intensities were corrected for variation of
n-flux in the sample and for gamma-ray absorption.

The yields of the three gamma-rays at 0.36, 0.96 and
1.33 MeV of Cu(n,n gamma) were measured with samples
of 5, 15, and 30 mm. The cross sections for the
various thicknesses agree within statistical errors.
